name: Trows
description: Trows are creatures from Shetland folklore, similar to trolls or dwarves in other Scandinavian traditions. They are often depicted as nocturnal beings who dwell in hills, mounds, and caves. Trows are known for their mischievous and sometimes malevolent nature, often playing tricks on humans, stealing livestock, and even abducting people, particularly children and young women. They are said to be skilled craftspeople, especially in the making of fine clothes and silver objects, but they are also considered ugly and deformed. Trows are sensitive to sunlight and will turn to stone if exposed to it. They are also repelled by the sound of church bells and the sight of steel. Some stories depict them as living in a complex social structure with kings and queens, while others portray them as solitary creatures driven by greed and malice. They are a significant part of Shetland's rich oral tradition and continue to influence local beliefs and customs. Their interactions with humans range from harmless pranks to dangerous encounters, highlighting the importance of respecting the supernatural world in Shetland culture.
subtitle: Mischievous hill-dwellers of Shetland folklore
appearance: Trows are generally described as short, ugly, and deformed creatures. They often have large noses, hunched backs, and long, tangled hair. Some accounts say they have pointed ears and sharp teeth. Their skin is typically grey or brown, and they are often covered in dirt and moss. They are said to wear ragged clothes and prefer to blend in with their surroundings.
habitat: Trows live in hills, mounds (known as 'trowie knowes'), caves, and other secluded places in the Shetland landscape. They are particularly associated with ancient burial sites and places considered to be liminal or between worlds.
behavior: Trows are primarily nocturnal creatures, avoiding sunlight whenever possible. They are known for their mischievous and often malevolent behavior, including playing tricks on humans, stealing livestock (especially milk), and abducting people. They are skilled craftspeople but are also greedy and envious of human possessions. They are said to be easily offended and quick to anger.

keyMyths.title: The Trow and the Fiddler
keyMyths.description: A talented fiddler is lured into a trow's mound and forced to play for their feasts. He eventually escapes by using his wit or a charm to break the spell, often returning with newfound musical skills.

keyMyths.title: Trowie Wives
keyMyths.description: Trows are said to sometimes abduct human women to become their wives, forcing them to live in their mounds. These women are often rescued by brave men who outsmart the trows.
keyMyths.source: The Folklore of the British Isles

sources.author: John Spence
sources.description: A collection of traditional Shetland folk tales, including many stories about trows.
sources.text: A collection of traditional Shetland folk tales, including many stories about trows.

sources.author: Katherine Briggs
sources.description: A comprehensive guide to fairies and other supernatural beings in British folklore, including information on Shetland trows.
sources.text: A comprehensive guide to fairies and other supernatural beings in British folklore, including information on Shetland trows.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings of Food and Drink
worshipPractices.description: While not strictly 'worship,' offerings of food and drink, especially milk and ale, were sometimes left out near trows' knowes (hills) to appease them and prevent mischief. This was less about reverence and more about practical appeasement to ensure the trows didn't sour milk or cause other troubles.

worshipPractices.name: Avoidance of Sacred Spaces at Night
worshipPractices.description: Certain locations like standing stones and burial mounds were considered vulnerable to trows at night. People would avoid these places after dusk to prevent encounters with the creatures, showing a respect (or fear) of their perceived power in these locations.

festivals.name: Winter Solstice Celebrations
festivals.description: Although not explicitly dedicated to trows, Winter Solstice celebrations in Shetland often involved rituals and precautions to ward off evil spirits during the darkest time of the year. These rituals likely offered some protection from the perceived threat of trows and other malevolent creatures.

cultural.modernLegacy: Trows continue to inspire artistic expression in Shetland, particularly in storytelling, music, and visual arts. Their mischievous nature and connection to the landscape make them a recurring theme in local folklore and a symbol of the region's unique cultural identity. They also appear in children's literature and local festivals, keeping the stories alive for new generations.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both are subterranean creatures associated with hills and caves, known for their craftsmanship and potential for mischief or malevolence towards humans.
Both are small, often solitary beings associated with hidden places and possessing a knack for trickery, sometimes involving treasure or craftwork.
While Domovoi are household spirits, some are associated with wilder places and share the Trows' characteristic of being mischievous if not properly respected or appeased.
Both are mischievous creatures who can be dangerous to humans if not treated with respect, and are associated with specific locations (Trows with hills, Kappa with water).

associations.name: Stone
associations.significance: Trows live within hills and mounds, making stone a fundamental part of their dwelling and existence.

associations.name: Grey
associations.significance: The color grey reflects the dim light of their underground homes and their often ambiguous moral nature.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: Trows are nocturnal creatures, making the moon a prominent symbol associated with their activities and emergence.

associations.name: Mischief
associations.significance: Trows are known for playing tricks and causing trouble, making mischief a core aspect of their character.

associations.name: Fungi
associations.significance: Fungi thrive in dark, damp environments, mirroring the underground habitats and hidden nature of the Trows.

associations.name: Seven
associations.significance: Seven is often associated with folklore and fairytales, aligning with the Trows' placement in Shetland mythology.

associations.name: Bat
associations.significance: Bats are nocturnal creatures dwelling in caves, mirroring the Trows' nocturnal habits and subterranean homes.

companions.name: Hill Folk
companions.description: Trows sometimes band together with other supernatural beings dwelling in hills for mutual protection or shared mischief. They occasionally form alliances to raid human settlements or protect their territory.

companions.name: Finfolk
companions.description: The Finfolk, sea-dwelling creatures with magical powers, are known to occasionally cooperate with Trows. This alliance is usually for trade or to further their respective goals of interfering with human affairs.

companions.name: Trowie Wives
companions.description: Trowie Wives are female Trows who often live in close proximity to their male counterparts. They share in the household duties and assist in both the mischievous and more serious endeavors of the Trows.

companions.name: Trowie Lads
companions.description: Trowie Lads are young male Trows who often accompany adult Trows. They help to maintain the home or cause mischief.

companions.name: Shetland Ponies
companions.description: Trows occasionally use Shetland ponies, either wild or stolen, as mounts for riding across the land at night. These ponies are often small and sturdy, well-suited to the rugged terrain.

extendedContent.title: Origin & Nature of the Trows
extendedContent.content:
The Trows of Shetland represent a localized adaptation of broader Norse and Scandinavian folklore concerning subterranean beings. Their origin is deeply intertwined with the landscape of Shetland, a remote archipelago strongly influenced by Norse culture. Unlike the towering trolls of mainland Scandinavia, Trows are often described as smaller, more akin to dwarves or hobgoblins. They are believed to be the remnants of an earlier race, possibly pre-Norse inhabitants, driven underground and transformed by their isolation and prolonged exposure to the hidden energies of the land. 

Their nocturnal habits are a defining characteristic, reflecting a fear of sunlight, which is often said to turn them to stone or cause them to explode. This aversion reinforces their connection to the liminal spaces between day and night, between the known world and the mysterious realm beneath. Their dwelling places, such as hills, mounds (known as 'howes'), and caves, are considered portals to their hidden world, further emphasizing their liminal nature. The physical descriptions of Trows vary, but they are generally depicted as ugly, misshapen creatures with long noses, wrinkled skin, and often wearing ragged clothing. These features contribute to their image as beings separate from and potentially hostile to humanity.

Scholarly interpretations suggest that the Trows may represent the anxieties and fears of a community living in a harsh and unpredictable environment. Their association with the underground and the night could symbolize the unknown dangers lurking beyond the boundaries of human control. The tales of Trows served as a way to explain misfortune, enforce social norms, and caution against venturing into dangerous or isolated areas.

extendedContent.title: Encounters in Myth: Tricks, Abductions, and Music
extendedContent.content:
Stories of encounters with Trows are rife with mischief and potential danger. A common theme is the Trow's penchant for playing tricks on humans, often involving stolen or misplaced objects, curdled milk, or livestock gone astray. These acts, while irritating, are often presented as relatively harmless pranks, highlighting the Trow's capricious nature. However, more sinister tales involve the abduction of humans, particularly children and young women. These abductions are often attributed to the Trows' desire for human companionship or their need for human skills, such as midwifery, which they lack themselves. 

The abducted are often replaced with a 'changeling,' a Trow child left in the human's place. The changeling is usually sickly, ugly, and constantly crying, making it easy to distinguish from the original child. These changeling narratives reflect anxieties surrounding infant mortality and the perceived vulnerability of young children to supernatural forces. Remedies for identifying and expelling changelings often involved harsh treatment, reflecting the desperation and fear of the parents. 

Music plays a significant role in Trow lore. Trows are often depicted as skilled musicians, particularly on the fiddle. Their music is said to be hauntingly beautiful but also dangerous, capable of luring humans into their realm or causing them to dance uncontrollably. There are stories of humans learning tunes from the Trows, but only at a great price, such as the loss of their sanity or the inability to play any other music. This association with music reinforces the Trow's ambiguous nature, capable of both enchanting and harming humans.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Meaning: Fear, Otherness, and the Unexplained
extendedContent.content:
The Trows, beyond being mere fairytale creatures, embody several layers of symbolism and meaning within Shetlandic culture. They represent the fear of the unknown, the anxieties of living in a remote and challenging environment, and the human tendency to project explanations onto the unexplained. Their nocturnal habits and subterranean dwellings symbolize the hidden dangers that lurk beyond the boundaries of human control and understanding. They serve as a reminder that the world is not entirely knowable or predictable, and that certain forces remain beyond human comprehension.

Furthermore, the Trows embody the concept of 'otherness.' Their grotesque appearance, their strange customs, and their ambiguous relationship with humans mark them as beings separate from and potentially threatening to the human community. This 'otherness' can be interpreted as a reflection of societal anxieties about those who are different or who challenge established norms. The stories of Trow abductions and changelings, in particular, highlight the vulnerability of the human family and the ever-present threat of disruption from the outside.

Finally, the Trows serve as a means of explaining the inexplicable. In a pre-scientific world, the Trows provided a framework for understanding misfortune, illness, and other unexplained phenomena. When crops failed, livestock died, or children fell ill, the Trows could be blamed, providing a sense of order and control in the face of chaos. This explanatory function of the Trows, while diminishing in modern times, continues to resonate with the cultural memory of Shetland, reminding us of the power of folklore to shape our understanding of the world.

extendedContent.title: Cultural Significance: Enduring Legacy and Modern Interpretations
extendedContent.content:
Despite the decline in traditional beliefs, the Trows continue to hold cultural significance in Shetland, albeit in a transformed manner. They are no longer feared as malevolent entities lurking in the hills, but rather serve as a reminder of Shetland's rich folklore and cultural heritage. The Trows have been embraced as symbols of Shetland identity, appearing in art, literature, and tourism initiatives. Storytelling festivals and local performances often feature Trow characters, keeping their stories alive for new generations. 

The enduring legacy of the Trows also reflects a broader appreciation for the power of folklore and mythology. In a world increasingly dominated by science and technology, the Trows offer a connection to a more imaginative and spiritual past. Their stories remind us of the importance of storytelling, the power of the human imagination, and the enduring fascination with the unexplained. Modern interpretations of the Trows often focus on their environmental symbolism, highlighting the importance of respecting the natural world and the dangers of disrupting the delicate balance of the ecosystem. 

Furthermore, the Trows have become a source of inspiration for contemporary artists and writers, who use them to explore themes of identity, otherness, and the relationship between humans and the environment. The Trows, therefore, continue to evolve and adapt, reflecting the changing values and concerns of Shetland society while preserving the essence of their ancient origins. Their presence in modern Shetland is a testament to the enduring power of folklore to shape our understanding of ourselves and the world around us. They remind us of the importance of preserving cultural traditions and celebrating the unique heritage of Shetland.

shortDescription: Trows are nocturnal, mischievous creatures from Shetland folklore, akin to trolls or dwarves, known for dwelling in hills and playing tricks on humans.
